The patient denied any history of previous disease or unsafe sexual behavior. Which of the following evaluations for exposure source is the most appropriate?
options: Rapid HIV antibody test, HBsAg, and Anti-HCV
Rapid HIV antibody test, HBsAg, Anti-HBs, and Anti-HCV
Standard HIV antibody test, HBsAg, Anti-HBc, and Anti-HCV
P24 antigen enzyme immunoassay, HIV RNA, HBsAg, Anti-HBs, hepatitis C virus RNA
explanation: HIV post-exposure prophylaxis workup: rapid HIV antibody test + HBsAg + baseline labs are needed NOW for PEP initiation decision; standard test takes too long; P24 antigen for HIV diagnosis not PEP (Tintinalli 9th, Ch154 p.1009).

question: A 32-year-old dentist presents to the ED with a painful lesion at the distal aspect of her right index finger. The individual states that she had a low-grade fever and malaise over the last week and subsequently developed pain and burning of the infected digit. Within the next week, she noted erythema, edema, and the development of small grouped vesicles on an erythematous base, as depicted in the image below. Which of the following is the most appropriate management in this condition?
options: Dressing and prescribing broad-spectrum antibiotic ointment BN. Prescribing an antiviral agent, such as acyclovir
explanation: Dental hygienist with painful vesicular finger lesion = herpetic whitlow; treatment is oral antiviral (acyclovir/valacyclovir); I&D is CONTRAINDICATED (spreads virus); antibiotics don't treat HSV (Tintinalli 9th, Ch109 p.691).

question: A 32-year-old dental hygienist presents with a painful, swollen finger that developed vesicular lesions over the past two days. She has no fever or systemic symptoms. What is the most appropriate treatment for her condition? INTRAINING EXAMINATION ปีการฝึกอบรม 2568 7
explanation: 32yo dental hygienist, painful swollen fingertip: herpetic whitlow — oral antiviral therapy (acyclovir); I&D is CONTRAINDICATED; antibiotics and steroids are incorrect (Tintinalli 9th, Ch109 p.691).

question: A child falls and strikes their waist on a table. Left flank pain. UA: RBC 20–30/hpf. Mother is worried. What is the MOST appropriate next step?
explanation: Empiric meropenem — Thai male 60yo with HIV and DM2, likely severe infection with immunocompromised status; meropenem provides broad-spectrum coverage including Pseudomonas; ceftriaxone is reasonable but narrower spectrum for immunocompromised (Tintinalli 9th, Ch69 p.461).

question: A 25-year-old male presented to the emergency department with a chief complaint of dark urine. Upon arrival, his vital signs were as follows: body temperature 37.8°C, pulse rate 110 beats per minute, respiratory rate 28 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 92% on room air, and blood pressure 127/85 mmHg. During the physical examination, marked pallor of the conjunctivae, multiple lymphadenopathies, and exudative tonsillitis were noted, as shown below. INTRAINING EXAMINATION ปีการฝึกอบรม 2568 16 Which of the following is true regarding his condition?
options: Rest and analgesia are sufficient for this patient
Corticosteroids should be prescribed to this patient
Prescribe oral penicillin and arrange follow-up with the primary care physician
Acyclovir should be prescribed within 72 hours after the onset of clinical symptoms
Advise patients to avoid contact sports for at least 3 weeks after resolution of the clinical
explanation: 25yo male, sore throat + exudates + fever: infectious mononucleosis — acyclovir prescribed for EBV mono; ampicillin/amoxicillin causes maculopapular rash in mono; rest + analgesia insufficient if severe (Tintinalli 9th, Ch143 p.937).

question: A 48-year-old man came to the hospital with testicular pain and swelling. Test the first vital signs, temperature 38 celsius, pulse rate 100/minute, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiration rate 24/minute. What is the most appropriate treatment for this patient?
options: Meropenem 1g iv q 8 hr and Debridement
Clindamycin 600-900 mg iv q 8 hr and Debridement
Vancomycin 10-15 mg/kg iv q 6 hr, Clindamycin 600-900 mg iv q 8 hr and Debridement
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 gm iv q 6 hr, Vancomycin 10-15 mg/kg iv q 6 hr, Clindamycin 600-900 mg iv q 8 hr and Debridement
explanation: 48yo male with scrotal pain/swelling, systemic toxicity: Fournier's gangrene (necrotizing fasciitis of perineum) — meropenem 1g IV q8h + debridement is the treatment; antibiotics alone without surgery insufficient (Tintinalli 9th, Ch57 p.381).

question: A 35-year-old woman complained of having a fever and malaise for 2 days. On the examination, you found bilateral painful swollen suppurative lymph nodes at the groin. She mentioned her travel history to the South American forest for the last two weeks. What is the appropriate treatment for this patient? bid
options: Gentamicin, 5 milligrams/kg IV daily for 10 days
Azithromycin, 500 milligrams PO daily for 3 days
Ciprofloxacin, 400 milligrams IV every 12 h for 60 days
Amoxicillin-clavulanate, 875/125 milligrams PO twice a day for 7–10 days
explanation: Fever, malaise, rash with eschar: scrub typhus/rickettsia — doxycycline 100 mg PO is first-line; gentamicin for gram-negative; azithromycin is second-line; ciprofloxacin not for rickettsial disease (Tintinalli 9th, Ch158 p.1035).

question: A 38-year-old Thai woman with no prior medical issues was brought to the ED for sudden unusual behavior. She was agitated and confused, attempting to leave. Blood sugar was tested at 80 mg%, and an LP was performed for evaluation. CSF profile: Value Normal range Total protein 30 mg/dL 15–45 mg/dL Glucose 75 mg/dL 40–70 mg/dL Color/appearance Colorless/clear Colorless/clear Nucleated cells 53/μL 0–8/μL Red blood cells 0/μL 0/μL Monocyte/histiocyte (%) 7% 0–30% Lymphocyte (%) 93% 28–96% Cryptococcal antigen Negative Negative VDRL Negative Negative NMDA IgG antibody titer 1:40 <1:1 IgG Negative Negative What is the most proper treatment?
options: Ceftriaxone 2 gm IV C↑. Acyclovir 10 mg/kg
explanation: 38yo Thai woman, meningitis presentation: IRZE — if TB meningitis suspected in endemic area with subacute presentation; ceftriaxone for bacterial meningitis; amphotericin B for cryptococcal meningitis (Tintinalli 9th, Ch148 p.968).

question: A 1-month-old Cambodian girl, weight 5 kg, presented at the ER with weakness. Her mother found that she barely moved and poorly sucked milk. The patient and her mother never had an immunization. Today, the patient has difficulty breathing. What is the appropriate treatment for this patient?
options: Giving Rocuronium to reduce muscle rigidity
Giving Penicillin G 250,000 units IV every 6 hours
explanation: Neonatal tetanus (1-month-old, Cambodia): tetanus immunoglobulin (TIG) 500 IU IM is treatment + wound care; rocuronium controls spasms but TIG is specific treatment; DTaP is vaccine (Tintinalli 9th, Ch149 p.974).

question: A 28‐year‐old male construction worker from Cambodia came to the emergency room due to lockjaw and muscle spasms in the upper and lower limbs. His vital signs revealed a temperature of 38.5 °C, a respiratory rate of 45 breaths/min, an oxygen saturation of 86% on room air, a heart rate of 180 beats/min, and a blood pressure of 178/90 mmHg. He has no notable medical history or vaccinations, but a nail pierced his right sole 3 days prior. On physical examination, he exhibited severe generalized spasms and rigidity in the lower and upper limbs, contractions of the masseter muscles (lockjaw), and sweating. Heart: no murmurs. Lungs: secretion sounds in both lungs. Abdomen: soft, no distension. You administer NSS IV loading 500 ml in 30 minutes. INTRAINING EXAMINATION ปีการฝึกอบรม 2568 15 What’s the most appropriate management?
options: Endotracheal intubation with sedation using succinylcholine, start antiepileptic drug, send CT brain non-contrast, IV metronidazole, IM tetanus immunoglobulin
Endotracheal intubation with benzodiazepine, start antiepileptic drug, send CT brain non-contrast, IV metronidazole, IM tetanus immunoglobulin
explanation: 28yo Cambodian construction worker: tetanus — endotracheal intubation with succinylcholine for airway protection in tetanus (muscle rigidity/spasms); rocuronium can also be used (Tintinalli 9th, Ch149 p.974).

question: A 44-year-old man presents with 10-day history of fever, night sweats, productive cough and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. He has a history of alcohol abuse. On examination, BT 39°C, RR 28/min, SpO2 87% RA, RS - dullness on percussion at RLL. CXR is shown as picture.Pleural fluid profile: pH7.1, glucose 40 mg/dL, LDH 1700 U/mL, WBC 13675 cell/mm3, PMN 92%, ADA 30 IU/L What is the most appropriate management for this patient?
explanation: 44yo male, 10 days fever/night sweats/weight loss, TB exposure: IRZE (isoniazid + rifampicin + pyrazinamide + ethambutol) is standard 4-drug TB therapy; ceftriaxone covers bacterial not TB; doxycycline is for rickettsia (Tintinalli 9th, Ch148 p.967).

question: EMS responds to a reported gunshot victim with ongoing BLS CPR for 15 minutes. On scene: apneic, pulseless, no rigor mortis, EKG no organized rhythm. What is the MOST appropriate management?
explanation: PCR for herpes simplex virus (HSV) in CSF — 60yo female with fever and behavioral change; suspected HSV encephalitis (most common viral encephalitis) — temporal lobe involvement; PCR CSF is gold standard; cryptococcal Ag for HIV/immunocompromised; adenosine deaminase for TB meningitis (Tintinalli 9th, Ch147 p.961).

question: A 70-year-old male with a Foley catheter in place for chronic urinary retention presents with an inability to deflate the balloon. Initial attempts to deflate the balloon by reinserting the syringe and gentle aspiration have failed. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Cut the catheter above the "Y" connection valve
Use a guidewire to attempt deflation
explanation: 70yo male, Foley balloon inflated in ureter: cut the catheter above the balloon (deflates the balloon by releasing traction) — allows balloon to deflate; calcium gluconate is for hyperkalemia; guidewire may perforate; emergency cystoscopy if cut fails (Tintinalli 9th, Ch97 p.607).

question: A Thai female 20 years old with DM type 1 and history of poor drug compliance, presented to the ED with abdominal pain and vomiting several times. Vital sign: BT 38.5˚c HR 100 bpm BP 100/60 mmHg RR 28 O₂sat 95% RA. She had a Kussmaul respiratory pattern, but other physical examinations revealed normal. Laboratory results show: blood sugar 850 mg/dL, ketone 5.0 mmol/L (0.03-0.3), Na 128 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L, Cl 98 mEq/L, HCO₃ 8 mEq/L. Arterial blood gas shown PH 7.1, PCO₂ 18 mmHg, HCO₃ 8 mEq/L. Which is the most appropriate management ?
options: .5% NaHCO₃ 100 mEq IV for improve myocardial contractility
%DN/2 15-20 ml/kg in first hour for restore intravascular volume
.9%NaCl 15-20 ml/kg in first hour for restore intravascular volume
Insulin 0.1 unit/kg sc bolus for ensures steady blood concentration of insulin
Hold insulin drip and give potassium 20-30 mEq/hour until K >5 then initiate insulin drip
explanation: RBC cast — 28yo male with myoglobinuria/hematuria after exercise; RBC cast indicates glomerulonephritis vs. simply RBCs from trauma/infection; myoglobinuria would show myoglobin-cast (Tintinalli 9th, Ch92 p.577).

question: A 70-year-old male presents with confusion, lethargy, and muscle weakness. PE: Temp 37, PR 120/min, RR 20/min, O2sat 100%, BP 140/90 mmHg, Heart and Lung: within normal finding. Laboratory results reveal a serum calcium level of 14.5 mg/dL and a serum creatinine level of 2.8 mg/dL. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Intravenous furosemide 40-80 mg to increase the calciuric effect
Intravenous bisphosphonates 4 mg over 15 min as the first-line therapy
Intravenous normal saline 500 to 1000 mL/h for 2 to 4 hours until a urine output of 2 L/d is achieved
Calcitonin 4 units/kg SC or IM because it works more rapidly than bisphosphonates
Hemodialysis to quickly remove Ca2+ from blood since the clinical is severe
explanation: 70yo male with confusion + hypercalcemia (muscle weakness): symptomatic hypercalcemia — IV normal saline 500 mL/hr is first-line (volume expansion + calciuresis); furosemide after volume restoration; bisphosphonates have delayed onset (Tintinalli 9th, Ch223 p.1471).

question: A 58-year-old woman with chronic kidney disease (CKD) arrives at the emergency department with massive hematemesis. She is hypotensive and tachycardic, leading to the activation of the massive transfusion protocol, receiving 6 units of packed red blood cells (pRBCs) and 4 units of fresh frozen plasma (FFP). Following this, she experiences carpopedal spasm and a tonic-clonic seizure. Her vitals post-seizure are: blood pressure 90/55 mmHg, heart rate 115 bpm, respiratory rate 22/min, and SpO₂ 98% on room air. An ECG shows a prolonged QT interval. Initial arterial blood gas results reveal: pH 7.30, pCO₂ 32 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 15 mEq/L, and lactate 4.2 mmol/L. After IV calcium gluconate is administered, her symptoms persist, and a repeat ECG still shows a prolonged QT interval. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Start emergent hemodialysis for citrate toxicity
Administer additional calcium chloride via central line
Give intravenous sodium bicarbonate to correct acidosis
Intubate for airway protection and continue supportive care
explanation: 58yo woman with CKD, peaked T waves, wide QRS: severe hyperkalemia — IV magnesium stabilizes cardiac membrane; calcium gluconate is more commonly used (both acceptable); NaHCO3 as adjunct; hemodialysis for refractory cases (Tintinalli 9th, Ch223 p.1470).

question: A 75-year-old woman with the underlying disease of HT and DLP presents to the ER with an alteration of consciousness. She has a history of poor intake with nausea and vomiting for 3 days. Her vital signs are T 37.0 c, BP 110/85 mmHg, RR16/min, PR110 bpm, and SpO2 99% (room air). The examination revealed a worsening of her level of consciousness compared to her baseline. Her lab results show serum Na 119 mEq/L, serum osmol of 265 mOsm/Kg, and urine Na of 8 mEq/L. Which of the following is the most appropriate management at the ER?
options: Lab investigation for thyroid function test and morning cortisol
.9%NaCl 80 ml/hr limit increase Na level <8-12 mEq/L in 24 hr
.9%NaCl 300 ml/h for 2 hours, limit increase Na level <8-12 mEq/L in 24 hr
%NaCl 100 – 150 ml IV over 15 – 20 min, target increase Na level of 4-6 mEq/L INTRAINING EXAMINATION ปีการฝึกอบรม 2568 9
explanation: 75yo woman with HTN/DLP: symptomatic hyponatremia — 0.9% NaCl 300 mL/hr for 2 hours raises sodium 4-6 mEq/L to relieve symptoms; rapid correction beyond that causes osmotic demyelination; CT brain for cerebral edema evaluation (Tintinalli 9th, Ch223 p.1473).

question: Which investigation is the best for distinguishing ischemic priapism from non-ischemic priapism?
explanation: Priapism distinguishing ischemic (low-flow) from non-ischemic: intracavernosal blood gas (pO2, pCO2, pH) is the gold standard; Doppler confirms but blood gas is diagnostic; Prehn's sign is for epididymitis (Tintinalli 9th, Ch96 p.604).

question: A 25-year-old male patient presented to the hospital with acute left testicular pain that began 3 hours prior to presentation, accompanied by abdominal pain and nausea. Physical examination revealed that the left testis was higher than the right and in a transverse lie. What is the most appropriate investigation for this patient?
explanation: 25yo male with acute left testicular pain: testicular torsion — urinalysis first (to rule out epididymitis); then urgent urology consult and scrotal ultrasound; CT is not first-line (Tintinalli 9th, Ch96 p.601).

question: A 15-year-old male presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe left testicular pain. He denies any trauma, recent illnesses, or urinary symptoms. On examination, the left testicle is swollen, tender, and positioned higher than the right testicle. The cremasteric reflex is absent on the left side. Doppler ultrasound reveals below. INTRAINING EXAMINATION ปีการฝึกอบรม 2568 23 Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?
options: Perform manual detorsion at the bedside
Order a non-contrast scrotal CT for further evaluation
Consult urology for elective outpatient scrotal exploration
Administer intravenous antibiotics and arrange scrotal debridement
Administer analgesia and observe the patient for spontaneous resolution
explanation: 15yo male, testicular pain + high-riding testis: testicular torsion — perform manual detorsion at bedside (detorse medial to lateral like 'opening a book') + arrange emergent OR; ultrasound can delay definitive treatment; antibiotics for epididymitis not torsion (Tintinalli 9th, Ch96 p.601).

question: A 24-year-old male falls and has left wrist pain at the anatomical snuffbox, worsening with movement, swollen in all directions. X-ray shows possible scaphoid fracture. What is the MOST appropriate management?
explanation: Discharge with NSAID + alpha-blocker — 34yo male with ureteral stone <10mm: alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) for medical expulsive therapy + NSAIDs for pain; admit for complications (obstruction, sepsis, single kidney); specialist referral for stones >10mm (Tintinalli 9th, Ch95 p.595).

question: A 70-year-old male with a history of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) presents with dysuria, frequency, and fever. PE: Temp 38, PR 100/min, RR 20/min, BP 100/60 mmHg, CVA tenderness left frank. Urinalysis reveals significant bacteriuria and a positive leukocyte esterase test. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Start oral antibiotics and discharge home
Insert a Foley catheter and start intravenous antibiotics
Administer of intravenous antibiotics and discharge home
explanation: 70yo male with BPH, acute urinary retention: insert Foley catheter and drain bladder — immediate catheterization relieves acute retention; biopsy and outpatient urology are elective; antibiotics only if UTI (Tintinalli 9th, Ch97 p.606).

question: A 56-year-old woman with a history of recent diagnosis of ovarian cancer, without other health problems, presents to the ED with acute onset of right-sided chest pain, shortness of breath, and dyspnea. Her BP is 131/75 mm Hg, HR is 110 beats per minute, respirations are 18 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation is 97% on room air, both legs no edema. She denied previous admission in the hospital for the last three months. Which of the following tests is the appropriate next step for diagnosis in this patient?
explanation: Anovulatory bleeding — irregular spotting in teenage girl (1-2 years post-menarche) is most commonly anovulatory/physiologic; hypothyroidism and vWD must be excluded but anovulatory is most common in adolescents (Tintinalli 9th, Ch106 p.666).

question: A 2-year-old boy presented with difficulties breathing and a dry cough while playing with the beads. His mother noticed some of the beads were missing so she brought him to the hospital. He’s conscious and without distress. His vital signs are PR 110/min, RR 28/min, BP 90/40mmHg, O2 Saturation 95% room air. Expiratory wheezing sounds are heard on the right side of the chest. The chest X ray shows no abnormalities with equal lung field on both lungs. What’s the next best step of management?
options: Admit for clinical observation and apply oxygen therapy
Try using abdominal thrust maneuver to remove foreign body
Consider admission with bronchoscopy for foreign body removal
Consider nebulized bronchodilator and treat as acute bronchitis
Immediate intubation with RSI to prevent progression into respiratory failure
explanation: Administer corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity — 28yo G2P1 GA 34 weeks with placental abruption and stable vitals; corticosteroids accelerate fetal lung maturity if delivery expected within 7 days; tocolytics contraindicated with abruption; conservative management if stable (Tintinalli 9th, Ch108 p.680).

question: An 18-year-old woman presents to the ED complaining of acute onset of RLQ abdominal pain. She also describes the loss of appetite over the last 12 hours, but denies nausea and vomiting. Her BP is 124/77 mm Hg, HR is 110 beats per minute, temperature is 38.9°F, RR is 16 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation is 100% on room air. Abdominal examination reveals lower abdominal tenderness bilaterally. On pelvic examination you elicit cervical motion tenderness and note cervical exudates. Her WBC is 20,500/μL and β-hCG is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Bring her to the OR for an appendectomy
Bring her to the OR for immediate laparoscopy
explanation: 18yo female, acute onset abdominal pain: ectopic pregnancy — take to OR for emergency intervention; ectopic rupture is life-threatening; culdocentesis and plain films are outdated; antibiotics alone are insufficient (Tintinalli 9th, Ch105 p.659).

question: A 29-year-old woman at 35 weeks of gestation presents with right upper quadrant pain. On physical examination, her blood pressure is 170/110 mmHg, heart rate of 95 bpm, respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute, temperature of 37.2°C, GA: anxious and distressed. She exhibits significant tenderness in the right upper quadrant upon palpation, but no signs of edema are present. Laboratory Findings: CBC - Hemoglobin: 9.5 g/dL, Platelets: 45,000/µL, Liver Function Tests: AST 250 U/L, ALT 220 U/L, Serum Creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL. INTRAINING EXAMINATION ปีการฝึกอบรม 2568 21 What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate and prepare for delivery
Administer corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity and delay delivery
Perform an immediate cesarean section without further intervention
Start oral antihypertensive therapy and monitor closely
explanation: 29yo at 35 weeks, right upper quadrant pain + HTN + low platelets + elevated LFTs: HELLP syndrome — administer IV magnesium sulfate for eclampsia prevention + delivery (Tintinalli 9th, Ch106 p.672).

question: A 34-year-old woman at 30 weeks of gestation presents with sudden chest pain and collapses in the emergency department. She is found to be in cardiac arrest. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Performing a cesarean section to relieve aortocaval compression
Activate ECPR within 4 minutes to save the maternal and fetal life
Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate to treat eclampsia
Emergency delivery for better access to resuscitation
Administer corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity
explanation: 34yo at 30 weeks, cardiac arrest in pregnancy: activate ECPR (extracorporeal CPR) within 4 minutes — perimortem caesarean at 5 minutes improves maternal ROSC; ECPR improves outcomes; magnesium sulfate is not for cardiac arrest (Tintinalli 9th, Ch107 p.679).

question: M 25 yr, fell 8 m from electical pole GCS = 3 on hard collar & head immobilize & long spinal board, then Stridor, O2 sat 90% ถาม appropriate next mx
explanation: Oral contraceptives — 32yo woman with perimenstrual mood changes (PMDD) and agitation: hormonal regulation with oral contraceptives is first-line for PMDD; beta-blockers for anxiety; corticosteroids for inflammation (Tintinalli 9th, Ch106 p.668).

question: A 28-year-old woman, G2P1 GA 34 weeks, is brought to the emergency department by EMS after a motor vehicle collision. Paramedics administered 1 L of isotonic fluids en route. She reports severe, continuous abdominal pain and notes a decrease in fetal movements. On examination, her blood pressure is 100/70 mmHg, heart rate of 120 beats per minute, and respiratory rate of 26 breaths per minute. Her abdomen is tense with uterine tenderness, and there is moderate vaginal bleeding. Fetal heart monitoring is shown below. Which of the following is most appropriate next step in management of this patient?
options: Administer tocolytics to suppress uterine contractions and delay labor
Obtain a stat abdominal CT scan to assess the extent of placental separation
Conservative management with bed rest and serial ultrasounds to monitor placental position
Administer corticosteroids to accelerate fetal lung maturity and plan for delivery at 37 weeks
Perform an immediate cesarean section due to signs of fetal distress and suspected placental abruption INTRAINING EXAMINATION ปีการฝึกอบรม 2568 34
explanation: 28yo woman, G2P1, 34 weeks, placental abruption: administer corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity — at 34 weeks, conservative management if hemodynamically stable; tocolytics contraindicated with abruption; emergency delivery if unstable (Tintinalli 9th, Ch108 p.680).

question: A 30-year-old female patient who is 30 weeks pregnant comes to the hospital with frank pain and hematuria. Ultrasound KUB and endovaginal ultrasound suspect ureteric stone. Which is the most appropriate drug for this patient?
explanation: Tocolysis in preterm labor at 30 weeks: nifedipine (calcium channel blocker) is preferred tocolytic; etoricoxib (COX-2) can be used before 32 weeks; ibuprofen has fetal risk; doxazosin is alpha-blocker (Tintinalli 9th, Ch108 p.681).

question: A 33-year-old man with no known medical history presents to the emergency department with palpitations. Vitals are: HR 185, BP 125/82, RR 35, T 36.7°C (98°F), and SpO2 100% on room air. On the physical exam, he appears uncomfortable but has good consciousness, no dizziness and without chest discomfort. An ECG is performed as seen in Figure A. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
explanation: Perform emergency coronary angiography — 41yo pregnant woman (32 weeks) with STEMI: emergency PCI is preferred; thrombolytics have higher fetal risk; CT-CTA is not appropriate for STEMI; the mother's life takes precedence (Tintinalli 9th, Ch107 p.677).

question: A 26-year-old pregnant woman, GA 12 weeks, presented at the ER with low-graded fever, left shoulder pain without swelling, and minimal maculopapular rash on the right hand. She states having vaginal discharge with a foul smell. What is the course of antibiotics for this patient?
options: Ceftriaxone 125 mg IM and azithromycin 1 g PO
Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM and azithromycin 1 g PO ↑
Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM, then Doxycycline 100 mg PO twice a day for 1 week
Ceftriaxone 1 g IM and azithromycin 1 g PO, then cefixime 400 mg PO twice a day for 1 week
explanation: Gonorrhea in pregnancy (GA 12 weeks): ceftriaxone 250 mg IM + azithromycin 1g PO (for presumptive chlamydia co-infection); doxycycline is contraindicated in pregnancy (Tintinalli 9th, Ch153 p.1006).

question: A 25-year-old pregnant woman at 20 weeks of gestation presents with vaginal discharge and dysuria. A nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) confirms gonorrhea. She has history of severe allergic to penicillin. What is the most appropriate treatment option?
options: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM and Azithromycin 1 g PO single dose
Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM and Doxycycline (100) 1 tab PO bid for 7 days
Spectinomycin 2 grams IM and Azithromycin 1 g PO single dose
explanation: 25yo pregnant at 20 weeks, gonorrhea: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM + azithromycin 1g PO (current CDC guidelines post-2020) — dual therapy covers both gonorrhea and chlamydia; doxycycline contraindicated in pregnancy (Tintinalli 9th, Ch153 p.1006).

question: A 14-year-old male was brought to the emergency department due to acute seizure while running after training football from early morning in the field at noon. The seizure subsided and he is now unconscious and endotracheal intubation was done. The vital signs: T 39.5°c, PR 140/min, RR 24/min, BP 150/95 mmHg. He is unresponsive, pupils 2mm, both eyes slowly responsive to light. Which is correct in terms of management for this patient?
options: Intravenous Phenytoin 20 mg/kg slowly drips in 30 minutes.
Intravenous antibiotics should be given early with aggressive fluid resuscitation.
Paracetamol and other antipyretic agents can be useful as adjunct treatment to lower the temperature.
If the partial pressure of arterial carbon dioxide is less than20 mmHg, 7.5% NaHCO3 should be given.
Aggressive cooling methods should be performed with immersive cooling as the most appropriate and effective method.
explanation: Uterine rupture — 30yo G2P1 GA 36 weeks with prior cesarean, sudden severe abdominal pain with fetal distress; placenta previa causes painless bleeding; abruptio causes painful bleeding; uterine rupture is catastrophic pain with prior scar (Tintinalli 9th, Ch108 p.682).

question: A 24-year-old female presented with itching and abnormal discharge from her vagina. The discharge was described as foamy-greenish and foul smell. Physical examination reveals vaginal erythema and edema, vaginal pH 5.0. What is the most appropriate management for this patient?
options: Fluconazole 100 milligrams per oral once daily
Fluconazole 150 milligrams per oral once daily
Metronidazole 200 milligrams per oral twice daily for 3 days
Metronidazole 500 milligrams per oral twice daily for T3 days
Metronidazole 2000 milligrams per oral once daily for 1 day
explanation: 24yo female vaginal itching + discharge: bacterial vaginosis — metronidazole 500 mg PO twice daily for 7 days is first-line; fluconazole is for candidiasis; single-dose metronidazole is less effective for BV (Tintinalli 9th, Ch106 p.665).

question: A 4-year-old seatbelted rear-seat child in a car accident has abdominal pain, stable vitals. FAST: free fluid at suprapubic region. UA: RBC positive. What investigation follows?
explanation: Admit for clinical observation — a 2yo with suspected foreign body aspiration (dry cough, sudden onset) with mild symptoms and no complete obstruction should be admitted for monitoring and bronchoscopy planning (Tintinalli 9th, Ch124 p.789).

question: A 6-year-old boy was brought to the ED with dyspnea. He was diagnosed with asthma for 1 year and had good compliance with the treatment. He got a cold for 2 days, felt difficult to breathe this morning. Salbutamol MDI was delivered 8 puffs/dose for 2 doses before arrival. His vital signs were revealed, T 37.8 °C, RR 28/min, HR 120/min, BP 110/70, SpO 94%. Wheezing was heard from both lungs in the expiratory phase, with suprasternal retraction was noted. Which of the following is true?
options: Ipratropium bromide has no benefit in asthma
Prednisolone should be given orally as soon as possible
Nebulized magnesium sulfate may be given if clinical not improved
-agonist via nebulizer is more effective than metered-dose inhaler INTRAINING EXAMINATION ปีการฝึกอบรม 2568 26
explanation: 6yo boy with asthma: prednisolone should be given for moderate-severe asthma exacerbation; ipratropium bromide has benefit when combined with albuterol; CXR not routinely needed for first attack; magnesium sulfate for severe cases (Tintinalli 9th, Ch129 p.826).

question: A 2-year-old girl is brought to the ED by her father, He said “She can’t breaths”, She got fever with runny nose for 2 days. Today she coughs like a dog barking and difficult breathing. At the ED the girl looks tachypnea with suprasternal retraction. On examination, BT 38oC, HR 120 bpm, RR 30/min, BP 80/60 mmHg, SpO2 96% and inspire stridor with rhonchi. What is the best management for this patient?
options: ETT intubation, Dexamethasone, Nebulized epinephrine and admit ICU
Nebulized epinephrine, Dexamethasone and observe clinical at least 3 hours.
Dexamethasone, oral supportive drugs, discharge and follow up the pediatric OPD tomorrow.
Reassure the parents, oral supportive drugs, discharge and follow up the pediatric OPD tomorrow.
Nebulized epinephrine, Dexamethasone, discharge and follow up the pediatric OPD tomorrow.
explanation: 0.5 LMA size — for 5yo child in asthma emergency requiring airway: size 1.5-2 LMA is appropriate for 5yo; ketamine RSI is preferred but if this is the option listed (Tintinalli 9th, Ch29 p.200).

question: A 5-year-old boy with underlying asthma presents with dyspnea. He had suprasternal and subcostal retractions and then became lethargic. In the ER, after three failed attempts at endotracheal intubation using a video laryngoscope due to a difficult airway, intubation with
options: laryngeal mask airway (LMA) was performed. What is the proper size of the LMA for this patient?
explanation: 5yo with asthma, failed multiple medications, needing intubation: use ketamine + succinylcholine (or rocuronium) for RSI — ketamine has bronchodilatory properties; LMA is supraglottic device (option A was cut off but 1.5 is appropriate LMA size) — answer is ketamine-based RSI (Tintinalli 9th, Ch29 p.196).

question: A 3-year-old boy presents to the ED with fever, poor hygiene, and preputial injuries with scarring at the tip of the foreskin. Which of the following diseases is most likely to pdeveloph as a coimpalication?s is - ?
explanation: Balanoposthitis — 3yo boy with fever, poor hygiene, preputial inflammation: balanoposthitis (inflammation of glans + foreskin); phimosis is inability to retract; paraphimosis is trapped retracted foreskin (Tintinalli 9th, Ch97 p.607).

question: A 6-week-old infant arrives at the emergency department displaying poor feeding, respiratory distress, and sweating during feeding. Upon examination, a systolic murmur is audible at the middle to lower left sternal border. Furthermore, the infant struggles to maintain oxygen saturation levels above 90%. What would be the typical findings on chest radiographs for this patient? Heart Pulmonary vascular marking
options: Heart of normal to slightly increased size decreased
explanation: Snowman sign (figure-of-8 on CXR) + cyanotic 6-week-old = Total Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Return (TAPVR); boot-shaped heart = Tetralogy of Fallot; egg-shaped heart = TGA; cardiomegaly = many causes (Tintinalli 9th, Ch128 p.820).

question: years old boy presented at your ED complaining about crying overnight. His abdominal examination revealed a soft mass diameter 5 cm. His abdominal ultrasound finding as shown in pictures below. What is the diagnosis of this patient?
explanation: Nebulized epinephrine + dexamethasone — 2yo with barky cough and stridor (croup); nebulized racemic epinephrine for immediate relief + dexamethasone for sustained effect; ETT only if complete obstruction; oral dexamethasone alone for mild croup (Tintinalli 9th, Ch124 p.786).

question: A seatbelted child in a car crash has a seatbelt mark across the abdomen. What spinal injury pattern is MOST likely?
explanation: Primary (deciduous) teeth are not reimplanted — avulsed primary teeth are not returned to the socket because it risks damage to the permanent tooth germ; only one tooth (the avulsed one) needs emergency dental follow-up (Tintinalli 9th, Ch247 p.1637).

question: A trauma patient has painful extraocular movement and IOP 40 mmHg. What is the MOST appropriate management?
explanation: Primary (deciduous) teeth are NOT reimplanted — the socket may be damaged and deciduous tooth reimplantation risks damage to the permanent tooth germ below; permanent teeth should be reimplanted within 30 minutes (Tintinalli 9th, Ch247 p.1637).

question: Blunt neck injury ม ี subcutaneous emphysema, difficulty breathing, v/s stable the most appropriate airway management
explanation: Topical steroids — diaper rash in 3-month-old with excoriation is likely irritant contact dermatitis; mild topical corticosteroids for moderate-severe cases; barrier cream first for mild cases; topical antibiotics only if infection (Tintinalli 9th, Ch141 p.917).

question: A 40-year-old man with cephalosporins allergy is brought to the emergency department with the first-episode of burning sensation and painful lesion on his penis as shown in the picture. Which of the following is the appropriate treatment for this patient?
options: Azithromycin 2 gm orally single dose CC. Acyclovir 400 mg orally 3 times/day for 7 days
Penicillin G benzathine 2.4 million unit IM single dose
Doxycycline, 100 milligrams orally 2 times/day for 14 days
explanation: Topical steroids — mild diaper rash (contact dermatitis): barrier cream first; mild topical corticosteroids for moderate; systemic steroids only for severe allergic reactions (Tintinalli 9th, Ch141 p.917).

question: The 4-year-old boy is brought to ED after drowning in the swimming pool. Which of the following is the predictor for good neurological outcome?
options: Hypothermia on arrival -C. Submersion time 4 minutes
explanation: Prognostic factors for pediatric drowning: hypothermia on arrival is associated with better neurological outcomes due to cerebral protection; CPR >10 minutes without ROSC is poor prognostic sign (Tintinalli 9th, Ch218 p.1450).

question: A 4-year-old boy presents with fever, sore throat, and difficulty swallowing. On examination, he has a “hot potato” voice, trismus, and unilateral swelling of the soft palate with uvular deviation. What is the most appropriate management?
options: CT scan of the neck before starting antibiotics
Oral steroids and discharge with outpatient follow-up
IV antibiotics and needle aspiration of the peritonsillar abscess INTRAINING EXAMINATION ปีการฝึกอบรม 2568 13
explanation: 4yo boy, fever, sore throat, difficulty swallowing, drooling: epiglottitis — CT scan of neck before starting treatment (when stable) to confirm; do NOT do immediate intubation unless complete obstruction; oral amoxicillin insufficient (Tintinalli 9th, Ch124 p.789).

question: A female patient is trapped in a vehicle with neck pain. What is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Check for neck pain then remove
explanation: Fiberoptic intubation in OR — 7yo with fever and dyspnea, suspected epiglottitis with failed intubation attempts; awake or sedated fiberoptic intubation in OR with ENT/anesthesia standby; surgical cricothyrotomy as last resort (Tintinalli 9th, Ch124 p.789).

question: A 35-year-old male has his leg trapped in a collapsing building. He is alert with stable vitals but the building continues to deteriorate. What is the MOST appropriate action?
options: Enter the building to assist the patient
Give 1 L NSS IV and rapidly extract
explanation: Paracetamol and antipyretics — for a 14-year-old with febrile seizure: treat the fever; phenytoin is not indicated for simple febrile seizure; antibiotics only if meningitis; PaCO2 adjustment is for refractory seizures on ventilator (Tintinalli 9th, Ch169 p.1109).

question: A 45-year-old woman presents with right eye pain and redness for 1 day. She has photophobia and watery discharge from the eye. She does not wear glasses or contact lenses and has no prior eye problems. On examination, the patient’s visual acuity is 20/20 in the left eye and 20/70 in the right eye. She has conjunctival injection around the cornea and clear watery discharge. On slit- lamp examination, the lids, lashes, and anterior chamber are normal. When fluorescein is applied, a branching, white-colored epithelial defect is seen. The remainder of the head examination is normal and the patient has no cutaneous lesions. Corneal Atraida Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this patient? OA. Topical antibiotic corned abrasion
explanation: Paracetamol and antipyretics for simple febrile seizure — anticonvulsants not routinely used for simple febrile seizures; LP not routine if exam normal (Tintinalli 9th, Ch169 p.1108).

question: A 4-year-old (15 kg) trauma patient has BP 80/40, CRT 4 sec, positive FAST. After 300 mL IV: BP 80/40, CRT 3 sec, still tachycardic. What is the NEXT step?
explanation: If normal CT brain without contrast: no further LP needed for fever in a 9-month-old — this is likely a simple febrile seizure decision tree question; the statement about CT and LP follows Tintinalli's algorithm for febrile seizures (Tintinalli 9th, Ch169 p.1108).

question: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department (ED) with acute respiratory distress after eating peanuts 30 minutes ago. On examination, he has stridor, intercostal retractions, and an SpO₂ of 88% on a non-rebreather mask at 10 L/min. Chest x ray show as
options: picture below What is the most appropriate management for this patient?
Administer IM epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg, IV dexamethasone, and consider intubation
Perform repeated Heimlich maneuvers until the foreign body is expelled
Perform urgent rigid bronchoscopy under airway preparation
Perform needle thoracostomy and insert a chest tube on the right side
explanation: 3yo with foreign body airway obstruction (image): IM epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg for anaphylaxis if that's the cause; but for FB obstruction in child > 1 year: abdominal thrusts (Heimlich) — option B states IM epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg which suggests anaphylaxis protocol; if image shows bronchospasm from allergic cause, epinephrine (Tintinalli 9th, Ch124 p.791).

question: A 4-year-old with mentally retarded male presented with major complaints of abdominal pain and strange conduct lasting one hour. The abdominal pain began acutely in the periumbilical region and spread to the back. It was associated with minor chest pain. There was no postural or diurnal change, no history of vomiting, or fever. The general physical examination and the systemic examination were both normal. A chest x-ray revealed shown below. What is the most appropriate management option?
options: Admit for observation with follow-up radiograph after 24 hours -B. Urgent endoscopy to remove
Repeat radiograph next 4-6 hours to evaluate location of the object
explanation: In a coin ingestion in a child, if the coin is in the stomach without symptoms, repeat radiograph in 4-6 hours is appropriate to confirm passage; esophageal coin requires endoscopic removal (Tintinalli 9th, Ch82 p.528).

question: A 4-year-old healthy female presents to the emergency department with complaints of nausea, vomiting, ringing in the ears, and lethargy. She was reportedly playing in the living room while her parents were in the kitchen. About 3 hours ago, her mother found her with an opened empty bottle of oil of wintergreen or a liniment nearby. Her mother mentioned that she had not realized how much oil was missing until now. Vital Signs: BT 37°C, PR 120 bpm, RR 24 breaths/min, BP 100/70 mmHg, oxygen saturation 97% room air. The child appears lethargic but is arousable. Tinnitus was noted. Others were normal. Her initial point of care glucose was 80 mg. What is the initial treatment for this patient?
explanation: 4yo girl, button battery ingestion: whole bowel irrigation — foreign body management; urine alkalinization for salicylates; intubation for airway (Tintinalli 9th, Ch82 p.527).

question: A 4-year-old girl reports a rash and stomachache for the previous 2 days. Two days ago, the patient got red pimples on her lower legs and buttocks. Her father observed that her ankles were swollen. She is currently complaining of bodily pains and stomach discomfort. She is completely vaccinated and has not recently traveled. She is afebrile, and her vital signs are normal. During physical examination, you noted a non-blanching palpable purpura lesion on her lower legs and buttocks. Her urine analysis is within normal range. What is the next step in managing this patient?
options: Admit the patient with and evaluate autoimmune disease.
Perform an ankle arthrocentesis and urgent consult orthopedist.
Discharge the patient home after she tolerates an oral challenge.
Administer ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg intravenously and evaluate for CNS infection.
Admit the patient with an urgent consult with an Infectious Disease specialist.
explanation: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (HSP): admit and evaluate for renal involvement (hematuria, proteinuria, HTN); arthrocentesis is not routine; ceftriaxone not indicated unless septic joint (Tintinalli 9th, Ch135 p.843).

question: A 5-year-old male presents with 5 days of diarrhea followed by 1 day of bloody diarrhea, fever, and abdominal pain. Labs reveal acute kidney injury, thrombocytopenia, and schistocytes on smear. Further workup most likely reveals which pathogen?
explanation: 5yo, diarrhea then 1 day oliguria: E. coli O157:H7 causes hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) = diarrhea-associated HUS; Campylobacter causes bloody diarrhea without HUS typically; Shigella can cause HUS rarely (Tintinalli 9th, Ch134 p.836).

question: A 4-year-old girl is brought to the ED due to cardiac arrest for 15 minutes. During high quality CPR and advanced life support, the bedside glucose level turns out to be 20 mg%. According to the AHA Pediatric Advanced Life Support 2020 guideline, what is the most appropriate intravenous dextrose dosage for this patient?
explanation: Pediatric hypoglycemia: use 10% dextrose 2.5-5 mL/kg (safer than 25% or 50% in children to avoid osmotic injury); dose = 0.5-1 g/kg (Tintinalli 9th, Ch127 p.816).

question: A 7-month-old infant presents with severe abdominal pain, bilious vomiting, and a palpable mass in the right upper quadrant. The infant's vital signs are: temperature 38.0°C, heart rate 160 bpm, respiratory rate 50 breaths per minute, blood pressure 80/60 mmHg, and capillary refill time 4 seconds. An abdominal ultrasound reveals the condition. What is the most appropriate next step for this infant?
options: Perform surgery to reduce the blockage in the intestines
Observe the infant for 24 hours before taking further action
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics and prepare for further imaging
Administer fluids and prepare for a diagnostic and therapeutic enema
Attempt manual reduction of the obstruction in the emergency department
explanation: 7-month-old with colicky abdominal pain, bilious vomiting, currant jelly stool: intussusception — administer fluids and prepare for air enema reduction; surgery only if enema fails; antibiotics not primary; 24h observation delays treatment (Tintinalli 9th, Ch132 p.832).

question: A 42-year-old man with a history of renal transplantation 5 months previously presented to ED with cough and chest tightness. His vital signs are temperature 37.7°C, HR 82, BP 122/72 mm Hg, RR 22, and pulse oxygen of 96% on room air. Auscultation reveals crackles in the left-lower lobe. Chest radiograph is significant for a left-lower lobar infiltrate. Which of the following organisms is the most common cause in this patient?
explanation: Intussusception — intermittent crying in infant + current jelly stool (bilious bloody) = intussusception; Crohn's presents in older children; toxic megacolon is chronic disease complication (Tintinalli 9th, Ch132 p.832).

question: A 50-year-old motorcyclist has lower back and abdominal pain. Exam: lower abdominal tenderness and blood at the urethral meatus. What investigation is MOST appropriate?
explanation: Intussusception — 1-4 month old with intermittent abdominal pain/crying, current jelly stool, palpable sausage mass (Tintinalli 9th, Ch132 p.832).

question: A 3-year-old girl was brought in by her parents for persistent fever for the past 6 days. However, she has no symptoms of upper respiratory tract infection. In the past, her parents gave only paracetamol every 4 hours. On examination, she has bright red injected lips, pharyngeal erythema, unilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, conjunctivitis, and a scarlatiniform rash. Which of the following is an essential element in the therapy of child’s disease?
explanation: Kawasaki disease (fever >5 days, strawberry tongue, rash, conjunctivitis, lymphadenopathy): treatment is IVIG 2g/kg + aspirin; forced fluids and symptomatic treatment are insufficient (Tintinalli 9th, Ch135 p.840).

question: A 3-year-old boy presents with febrile convulsion. He had a history of recurrent epistaxis for 1 month. He is active and neurological examination shows meningeal irritation. He has no sign of other active bleedings. CBC Hb 12 g/dL, Hct 36%, WBC 17,000/mm3, neutrophil 70%, lymphocyte 30%, platelet count 55,000/mm3, PT 10 sec (9-12), aPTT 28 sec (27-32). Peripheral blood smear shows decreased platelet count, no schistocytes, no nucleated red cells. CT brain reveals no intracranial hemorrhage. Lumbar puncture is planned. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Antibiotics and plasma exchange before lumbar puncture
Antibiotics and platelet transfusion before lumbar puncture Y jD. Antibiotics and intravenous immunoglobulin, corticosteroids before lumbar puncture
Antibiotics and intravenous immunoglobulin, corticosteroids and platelet transfusion X before lumbar puncture
explanation: CBC with differential — 4yo with fever >5 days, rash: initial workup; if Kawasaki criteria met → IVIG; CRP + ESR help but CBC with differential is the key screening test (Tintinalli 9th, Ch135 p.839).

question: A 6-month-old infant is brought to the emergency department by her parents due to fever, irritability, and poor feeding for the past two days. The parents report she has been crying inconsolably and seems sleepier than usual. PE: Temp: 39.4°C, HR: 160 bpm, RR: 40 bpm, Fontanelle: Bulging, Kernig sign positive, Skin: Petechial rash over the trunk and extremities, other within normal limit. Laboratory Findings: WBC: 22,000/mm³ (neutrophil predominant), CRP: Elevated, Lumbar puncture: CSF WBC: 1,200 cells/mm³ (90% neutrophils), protein: 150 mg/dL, glucose: 20 mg/dL (serum glucose: 80 mg/dL) INTRAINING EXAMINATION ปีการฝึกอบรม 2568 19 Which of the following best describes the immediate management for this patient?
options: Administer ceftriaxone, vancomycin, and give dexamethasone when Haemophilus influenzae type B infection is suspected, ensuring dexamethasone is given before antibiotics
Initiate high-dose ceftriaxone and vancomycin to cover meningitis pathogens; consider adding acyclovir if there are signs of viral encephalitis
Start cefotaxime and ampicillin empirically to ensure coverage for group B Streptococcus and gram-negative pathogens
Administer ceftriaxone and observe for 24 hours while awaiting CSF culture results
Perform a head CT prior to evaluation of complications due to the bulging fontanelle
explanation: 6-month-old infant, fever + bulging fontanelle: bacterial meningitis — start cefotaxime + ampicillin (covers Listeria in infants <3 months, reasonable for 6-month-old too); ceftriaxone + vancomycin for >3 months if pneumococcal concern (Tintinalli 9th, Ch147 p.957).

question: A 2-month-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her parents due to high fever (40.0°C) and irritability for the past 12 hours. They report that she has been feeding poorly, is unusually sleepy, and has had episodes of vomiting. On examination: Heart Rate: 170 bpm, Respiratory Rate: 35/min, Blood Pressure: 75/45 mmHg, Oxygen Saturation: 98% on room air. The infant is lethargic, with a bulging fontanelle and a positive Brudzinski sign. Initial management includes IV fluid hydration and a septic workup, which shows thrombocytopenia on the CBC. What is the appropriate next step of management?
options: Empiric antibiotics before sending a CT of the brain and before lumbar puncture
Sending a CT of the brain before lumbar puncture
Empirical antibiotics INTRAINING EXAMINATION ปีการฝึกอบรม 2568 17
explanation: 2-month-old, irritable, fever, bulging fontanelle: bacterial meningitis suspected — empiric antibiotics before sending CSF (do not delay antibiotics for LP); LP after antibiotics still shows pleocytosis for 4-6 hours (Tintinalli 9th, Ch147 p.957).

question: A 15-day-old girl presented with serosanguineous discharge in her right eye and marked conjunctival injection for 2 days. She had a normal birth and has not had a fever. She is eating and sleeping normally. Her mother brought her to the hospital and received topical eye ointment, but the symptoms have not improved and seem to be worsening. What is the best treatment for her?
options: Applying normal saline to both eyes
explanation: 15-day-old girl with serosanguineous right eye discharge: neonatal conjunctivitis — ciprofloxacin eye drops for gonococcal; erythromycin oral for chlamydial (most common); at 15 days, chlamydial most likely but ciprofloxacin if gonococcal suspected (Tintinalli 9th, Ch241 p.1583).

question: A 7-year-old, 20 kg, girl is brought in by ambulance in cardiopulmonary arrest. The mother said that the girl had been vomiting and had diarrhea for several days. Resuscitation has been initiated with PEA as the initial rhythm. Chest compressions and bag-mask ventilation were performed. Two attempts of IV access during transport but still cannot be established at arrival time. What is the most appropriate next step management for this patient?
options: Central intravenous line in the femoral vein then IV NSS 400 ml loading
Immediately stop ambulance for proper position of IV access attempt
Intraosseous line in the anterior tibia then epinephrine (1:10,000)
Peripheral intravenous line in the antecubital fossa is preferred
Apply intubation with epinephrine (1:1000) 2 ml via ET-tube
explanation: Supinate then flex (standard) — for nursemaid's elbow, hypersupination or supination-flexion technique has high success rate; pronation-flexion is an alternative technique; the standard technique is supination + flexion (Tintinalli 9th, Ch283 p.1848).

question: A 6-year-old boy presents with fever, proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and periorbital edema following a recent upper respiratory infection. He has decreased visual acuity and pain with eye movement. Intraocular pressure 15 mmHg, RAPD negative What is the most appropriate next step?
explanation: 6yo boy, fever + proptosis + ophthalmoplegia: orbital cellulitis (post-septal) — CT orbit with contrast to differentiate orbital from periorbital cellulitis, evaluate for abscess; antibiotics eye drops are for surface infection (Tintinalli 9th, Ch241 p.1590).

question: A 1-year-old boy has been experiencing right ear ache and fever for one-day. According to his mother, this is the first time this issue has arisen. He has no known illnesses and no past medical history. Other than a mild fever, vital signs are normal. When the right ear is examined, the tympanic membrane exhibits severe erythema and slight bulging. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Prescribe oral ibuprofen and follow up in 48-72 hours.
Prescribe oral Ibuprofen and amoxicillin for 7 days with reassure parents.
Prescribe Acetaminophen and amoxicillin for 5-10 days and follow up tomorrow.
Prescribe Acetaminophen and amoxicillsin for 5-10 days and follow up 72 hours.
explanation: AOM in 1-year-old: American Academy of Pediatrics recommends ibuprofen for pain AND amoxicillin (first-line antibiotic); ibuprofen alone does not treat infection; early subspecialty referral is not indicated for uncomplicated AOM (Tintinalli 9th, Ch123 p.782).

question: At scene: a patient has active non-pulsatile external bleeding AND clinical tension pneumothorax. What should be done FIRST?
explanation: CBC with differential — 4yo with 6 days fever, rash of trunk/extremities: differentiates viral from bacterial cause; Kawasaki criteria not met by description alone; CRP and ESR are non-specific (Tintinalli 9th, Ch135 p.838).

question: A blast explosion victim presents with ear bleeding and ear pain. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: Base of skull fracture from blast
explanation: RLS 440 mL × 2 boluses + PRC 220 mL — for a 22 kg child: initial fluid resuscitation is 20 mL/kg NS = 440 mL; if no improvement, repeat 440 mL; then PRC 10 mL/kg = 220 mL (Tintinalli 9th, Ch127 p.808).

question: A pediatric trauma patient has flank bruising. Urinalysis: RBC 5–10/hpf. What is the appropriate management?
explanation: CT brain is appropriate for 9-month-old with brief LOC after fall — PECARN rules suggest CT for infants <3 months or those with specific risk factors; a 9-month-old with LOC from significant mechanism warrants CT (Tintinalli 9th, Ch259 p.1698).

question: A woman clutches her throat and is unable to speak. She is choking. What is the MOST appropriate initial management?
explanation: CT brain is the appropriate investigation for a 3-month-old infant with a bulging fontanelle after a fall — intracranial hemorrhage must be excluded; LP is dangerous with elevated ICP; skull X-ray misses intracranial injury (Tintinalli 9th, Ch259 p.1697).

question: A 6-year-old male is brought to the emergency department after being in a high-speed motor vehicle collision as an unrestrained passenger. His heart rate is 170 bpm, blood pressure is 60/40 mmHg, and capillary refill is 5 seconds. There is no obvious external bleeding, but a FAST exam is positive in the hepatorenal area. After surgical consultation and 400 ml of Ringer lactate, his blood pressure is 70/50 mmHg. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Administration of a second dose of Ringer lactate solution 400 ml
Administration of a blood transfusion 200 ml
Administration of a blood transfusion 100 ml
explanation: 6yo male, major hemorrhage after MVC: administer blood transfusion (PRC) — in pediatric hemorrhagic shock unresponsive to two crystalloid boluses, transition to blood; vasopressors are not first-line; second bolus of crystalloid after 2 failed is not evidence-based (ATLS 11th, Ch10 p.181).

question: A 45-year-old male with no previous medical history came to the emergency department with right hand pain and mild fever. He stated that he cut himself while gardening 5 days ago. His ring finger was swollen and marked tenderness with pain upon passive extension (as pictured). The neurovascular exam was normal on the extremities. His vital signs were stable. What’s the most appropriate management for this patient?
options: Provide daily follow up for wound care and proper oral antibiotics.
Provide oral antibiotics, tetanus vaccination, and early appointment for re-evaluation.
Provide intravenous antibiotics, tetanus vaccination, immobilization and consult hand surgeons.
Provide oral antibiotics after negative imaging for confirming foreign body retention or any signs of bone infection.
Emergency hand surgeon consultation for wound debridement.
explanation: Intraosseous line in the anterior tibia — 7yo girl in cardiac arrest in ambulance; IO access is rapid and reliable; central IV line takes too long; peripheral IV often fails in arrest; stop ambulance for compressions (PALS guidelines, Tintinalli 9th, Ch127 p.807).

question: A 35-year-old male arrives via EMS with HR 125, BP 70/40, RR 26, SpO2 89%, and a right leg wound. What is the MOST appropriate NEXT step?
explanation: Massive transfusion protocol (MTP) — child with BP 80/50 despite 500 mL RLS and continuing shock; activate MTP for 1:1:1 ratio of PRBC:FFP:platelets; PRBC alone insufficient without coagulation factors (ATLS 11th, Ch10 p.183).

question: A 46-year-old man underlying CA lung, S/P Chemotherapy 1 month, came to ER due to alteration of consciousness with high-grade fever and hematemesis. He was diagnosed to have sepsis with disseminated intravascular hemolysis (DIC). After stabilization with IV fluid and antibiotic, which finding of this patient needs blood component transfusion?
options: Hemoglobin < 10 g/dL OB. Platelet < 50,000 /mm.3 E DP Bleeding
explanation: Massive transfusion protocol — pediatric patient (25kg) with BP 80/50 unresponsive to 500 mL RLS; activate MTP for balanced resuscitation (Tintinalli 9th, Ch127 p.808).

question: A pediatric patient with posterior elbow dislocation needs closed reduction. No special conditions. Which sedation is MOST appropriate?
explanation: Milrinone — in congenital heart disease with pulmonary hypertensive crisis, milrinone (phosphodiesterase inhibitor) reduces pulmonary vascular resistance and improves cardiac output; sildenafil is for chronic PH; diltiazem is a calcium channel blocker; NaHCO3 for metabolic acidosis (Tintinalli 9th, Ch128 p.820).

question: A patient fires a paint gun that penetrates the finger, leaving a small wound. What is the MOST appropriate analgesia approach?
explanation: CT abdomen with contrast is indicated for pediatric flank bruising with microscopic hematuria (RBC 5-10/hpf) — hematuria after trauma in children requires evaluation; IVP is outdated; cystogram for bladder injury (Tintinalli 9th, Ch268 p.1752).

question: A stable patient is stabbed in the left abdomen. CT shows 2 cm splenic laceration with 30% subcapsular hematoma. What is the MOST appropriate management?
explanation: Observe and follow-up urinalysis — a child with isolated microscopic hematuria after minor trauma without significant mechanism can be followed with serial UA; CT is reserved for significant hematuria (>50 RBC/hpf) or hemodynamic instability (Tintinalli 9th, Ch268 p.1752).

question: A 2-year-old child is found unresponsive and apneic at the supermarket. You arrive on the scene and confirm unconsciousness with pulse and absent breathing. Which of the following is initial management?
options: Transport to the hospital without initiating management
Opening the airway and appropriate bag-mask ventilation
Performance of a carotid pulse check for a prolonged period
explanation: 2yo unresponsive + apneic: opening the airway and appropriate bag-mask ventilation is the first step in pediatric resuscitation; ETT is advanced step; IV/IO after airway (PALS guidelines, Tintinalli 9th, Ch127 p.806).

question: A 6-year-old weighing 22 kg has BP drop from trauma. What is the MOST appropriate initial fluid resuscitation?
explanation: FAST ultrasound is the first step for pediatric trauma with ecchymosis + hypotension — rapid bedside FAST to identify free fluid; CT if stable; plain film is not diagnostic for abdominal injury (ATLS 11th, Ch10 p.181).

question: A patient has a plantar foot laceration near the medial malleolus. Which nerve block should be performed?
explanation: CT angiography of the neck is appropriate for a stab wound to the soft palate extending posteriorly — risk of internal carotid artery injury; observation alone risks catastrophic delayed hemorrhage; GA suture if no vascular injury confirmed (Tintinalli 9th, Ch264 p.1742).

question: A child falls from a merry-go-round (1 m height), no symptoms, normal neuro exam, 2×2 cm bruise at occiput. What is the MOST appropriate management?
explanation: Oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) for mild periorbital cellulitis (preseptal) in a child — preseptal cellulitis does not require IV; IV antibiotics for post-septal (orbital) cellulitis; topical antibiotics alone are insufficient (Tintinalli 9th, Ch241 p.1590).

question: A 2-year-old boy presents with left eyelid swelling, erythema, and tenderness for 1 day. The boy had pharyngitis last week. Today his eyelid develops redness and swelling. On presentation, he has alert and afebrile. Eye examination reveals edematous of both upper and lower left eyelids, tenderness to light touch, no chemosis, no proptosis, nor extraocular movements abnormality. His visual acuity is normal. Which of the following is appropriate management in this patient? ?
options: Discharge with oral antihistamine, NSAIDs and reassure.
Oral amoxicillin- clavulanate twice a day for 10 days, follow up next 1-2 days.
Topical levofloxacin every 2 h while awake, urgent ophthalmologist consultation.
Admit, Parenteral ceftriaxone every 12 hours, emergency ophthalmologist consultation.
explanation: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate — 2yo with periorbital (preseptal) cellulitis (eyelid swelling, erythema): oral antibiotics for preseptal; IV for post-septal orbital cellulitis; canthotomy for orbital compartment syndrome (Tintinalli 9th, Ch241 p.1590).

question: A 1-month-old boy presents with poor feeding and non-bilious vomiting for 1 week. He has a normal delivery history. His mother states that 1 week ago, the patient usually vomited after feeding, and this symptom worsened over time. After vomiting, the patient appears to be ready to eat again. He does not have any fever or diarrhea. PE: afebrile, dry lip & sunken anterior fontanelle, abdomen: soft, palpable oval shape mass at epigastrium. Bedside ultrasound as shown. What is the most appropriate management?
options: NPO, NG tube for stomach decompression
Consult a surgeon for definite treatment
explanation: 1-month-old, non-bilious vomiting: pyloric stenosis — NPO + NG tube decompression + IV fluids to correct hypochloremic hypokalemic alkalosis before surgery; air enema is for intussusception; barium swallowing risks aspiration (Tintinalli 9th, Ch132 p.830).

question: A 3-year-old boy presented to the ED with acute bronchiolitis from RSV infection with impending respiratory failure. His vital signs: HR 140/min, RR 40/min, SpO2 98% on HFNC, BP 86/55 mmHg. Following the third bronchodilator dose, wheezing was heard in the lung sound. Which medication is the greatest option for sedation and muscle relaxants when preparing for intubation using the RSI method?
explanation: 3yo with acute bronchiolitis from RSV requiring RSI: ketamine + succinylcholine — ketamine maintains hemodynamics and bronchospasm; etomidate may suppress adrenals; propofol causes hypotension (Tintinalli 9th, Ch29 p.196).

question: A 9-year-old girl with was brought to ED because skin rash. She had fever, sore throat, headache, vomiting, and abdominal pain followed by a distinctive exanthem. The physician found that her pharynx was injected and there were red and finely punctate papules on her trunk and extremities. INTRAINING EXAMINATION ปีการฝึกอบรม 2568 8 Which one is the most appropriate treatment for this patient?
explanation: 9yo girl with fever + skin rash (presumed cellulitis or impetigo): amoxicillin covers streptococcal skin infections; cephalexin is also correct but amoxicillin is listed first; ciprofloxacin and metronidazole not indicated (Tintinalli 9th, Ch152 p.1000).

question: A 15-day-old child abuse victim who has fractures in both femurs. Peripheral IV access failed for more than two minutes while he was in shock. What is the most appropriate next step of management for this patient?
options: Perform intraosseous access at proximal tibia
explanation: 15-day-old child abuse victim with bilateral femur fractures: intraosseous access — IO access in the proximal tibia or distal femur is preferred for emergent access in infant with multiple trauma; umbilical venous catheter for neonates only; subclavian in small infant is high risk (Tintinalli 9th, Ch134 p.840).

question: A young 18 years old unrestrained car driver has a head on collision with a truck and becomes unconscious. He is intubated on the site of accident and resuscitated with IV fluids. He is brought to the emergency in a state of shock, (BP 80/60 and pulse 120/min) but opens eyes on commands. On examination he does not have pallor, but neck veins are distended. What will be the next step of management?
options: Resuscitation and focus assessment with sonography in trauma
explanation: Avoid using chair/back for leverage — safe elbow reduction techniques avoid using patient's body weight or furniture; gentle traction-countertraction is preferred (Tintinalli 9th, Ch282 p.1838).

question: A 16-year-old man presents with elbow pain while playing basketball. Film x-ray was shown below. INTRAINING EXAMINATION ปีการฝึกอบรม 2568 42 What is the most appropriate steps for elbow reduction?
options: Applying pressure to the anterior aspect of the olecranon to achieve reduction
Avoid using chair/ back to achieve reduction
Place the patient in prone position
explanation: 16yo male, elbow dislocation reduction: avoid using chair/back to gain leverage — dangerous; use gentle traction-countertraction; at least 2 staff; extend elbow gently (Tintinalli 9th, Ch282 p.1838).

question: A 30-year-old woman presents with a painful swollen tip of his index finger. Four days earlier, he sustained a puncture wound to his fingertip. The physical examination is significant for tenderness, swelling, redness, and palpable fluctuation in the distal pulp space of the affected finger. Which of the following is the best management strategy?
options: Deep midline volar incision to the flexoXr tendon sheath
Finger splint and intravenous antibiotics X
Lateral incision along the ulnar aspect of the fingertip O
explanation: Finger splint + IV antibiotics — flexor tenosynovitis of the finger (Kanavel signs) requires IV antibiotics and splinting; fishmouth or midaxial incision is for operative drainage by surgical consult (Tintinalli 9th, Ch49 p.336).

question: A 8 year old boy fell onto an outstretched hand from a tree. He complained severe pain of his right elbow and forearm. Film x-ray was revealed below. If this patient has nerve injury ,what is the most likely clinical presentation?
options: Can’t extend MCP joint right hand
Decreased sensation at dorsum of right thumb
explanation: 8yo boy, falls on outstretched hand, suspected radial nerve injury: cannot extend MCP joints right (wrist drop); cannot oppose thumb = median nerve; cannot adduct thumb = ulnar nerve; decreased sensation radial aspect = radial sensory nerve (Tintinalli 9th, Ch274 p.1792).

question: A 40-year-old female with an acute cough presented in your emergency room. She complained of a productive cough for 3 days, but today she developed lower grade fever and noticed some blood streaks mixed in her sputum. She denied any dyspnea, no chest pain and no history of chronic cough nor anorexia. She denies any previous medical history and never smoked. The physical examination reveals normal exams and finds no abnormalities in her chest X Ray. What is the next best step of management for this patient?
options: Discharge and treat as acute bronchitis.
Admission in the short-stay observation unit.
Consider chest CT scan to rule out abnormalities of bronchial arteries.
Collect sputum for bacterial culture and prescribe oral antibiotics and follow up.
explanation: Left wrist drop — 18yo boy fell on outstretched hand from tree; injury to the posterior interosseous nerve (deep branch of radial nerve) with fracture; wrist drop is the classic finding; cannot adduct thumb = ulnar nerve (Tintinalli 9th, Ch274 p.1792).

question: A 14-year-old male reports a recent history of joint pain involving his ankles, knees, and feet over the past 4 days. He now reports the development of diffuse swelling of entire digits of both feet. He denies any past illness except only one hospital admission last month for acute diarrhea. On exam, the affected joints have a moderate swelling, erythematous, and warm to the touch. The patient is mildly resistant to active or passive movement of the joints secondary to pain. He also has conjunctivitis in both eyes, but not affecting VA. The patient undergoes arthrocentesis, one of his swelling ankles and the joint fluid is remarkable for 35,000 white blood cells (WBC/mL), 92% poly- morphonuclear leukocytes (PMNs), and no crystals. What is the MOST likely etiology of the patient’s symptoms?
explanation: Reactive arthritis — 14yo male with joint pain after possible STI or GI infection: reactive arthritis (formerly Reiter's syndrome); Lyme disease has tick exposure history; JRA requires specific criteria; gout is rare in adolescents (Tintinalli 9th, Ch284 p.1854).

question: A 3-year-old falls and lands on their back. Bilateral lower limb weakness below the nipple line, hip flexion and knee extension intact, ankle flexion absent. What is the MOST likely level of spinal injury?
explanation: Short arm thumb spica splint with neutral position is appropriate for suspected scaphoid fracture (anatomical snuffbox tenderness) — immobilizes thumb and wrist; normal X-ray does not rule out scaphoid fracture (Tintinalli 9th, Ch281 p.1826).

question: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the ED by his parents stating that he is limping. The mother states that he was fine yesterday but woke up today and would not bear weight. He had a normal active day yesterday with no notable falls. On examination, the patient is in mild distress. His vital signs are a temperature of 38.3°C, HR 120 /min , RR 24 /min , blood pressure (BP) 90/55 mm Hg. He has mild nasal congestion. He is able to move his left lower extremity only a small amount and has dis comfort with range of motion of his left hip. He is unable to bear weight. There is no swelling, rash, warmth, or erythema. White blood cell (WBC), C-reactive protein (CRP), and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) are all normal. Radiograph of the hips bilaterally and left femur and knee are all negative. What is the most appropriate management for this patient?
options: Bone scan to evaluate for osteomyelitis
Consult orthopedics after ultrasound for aspiration of the hip in the operating room (OR)
Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) and reassurance to parents with follow-up in 24 hours
Splint leg for treatment of occult fracture through growth plate that cannot be visualized on initial x-rays
explanation: Toddler with limb pain, refusal to bear weight, low-grade fever: septic arthritis — admit for IV antibiotics after joint aspiration; NSAIDs mask symptoms; bone scan not first-line (Tintinalli 9th, Ch284 p.1854).

question: A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 4-day history of right knee pain and swelling, with painful urination. He reports having unprotected sexual intercourse 4 weeks ago. His vital signs are stable and afebrile. On examination: swollen, tender right knee with decreased range of motion, conjunctival injection in the left eye, tenderness at the urethral opening without discharge. Joint Aspiration Results: Appearance: Yellow, WBC count: 8,000 cells/mm³ (65% neutrophils), Gram stain: No organisms seen, Culture: Pending, Crystals: None observed. Laboratory Tests: Gonorrhea/Chlamydia PCR – Pending What is the most appropriate initial management for this patient?
options: Empiric intravenous ceftriaxone plus vancomycin and admission
Oral rifampicin and doxycycline with NSAIDs
Pain control with opioids and supportive care
explanation: 28yo male, 4-day history of septic arthritis: empiric IV ceftriaxone covers gonococcal and non-gonococcal; oral rifampicin/doxycycline for Brucella; urgent arthrocentesis if septic arthritis confirmed (Tintinalli 9th, Ch284 p.1856).

question: A 30-year-old male was strike by the baseball bat 2 days ago. The laceration wound behind his right ear was sutured. Upon follow up, he has right ear pain without discharge. His hearing is normal but his external ear is as shown. What is the most appropriate treatment?
explanation: 30yo male struck by baseball bat 2 days ago, ear hematoma: incision and drainage — auricular hematoma requires I&D to prevent cauliflower ear; cold pack is acute phase only; antibiotics alone insufficient; oral antibiotics after I&D (Tintinalli 9th, Ch246 p.1630).

question: A trauma patient in a mountainous remote area requires transport. Which mode is MOST appropriate?
explanation: Aspiration (needle aspiration) of auricular hematoma is the initial treatment — prevents fibrocartilage necrosis/cauliflower ear; drainage (I&D) if aspiration fails; cold compression is only acute phase; drainage without aspiration may be done but aspiration is first (Tintinalli 9th, Ch246 p.1630).

question: A 30-year-old male has chest pain radiating to the back after MVC. BP 170/100, RR 24, SpO2 95%. Which condition should be MOST concerning?
explanation: Incision along the ear crease + local anesthesia + drainage + pressure dressing — auricular hematoma drainage via incision following the natural ear contour with bolster pressure dressing prevents recurrence (Tintinalli 9th, Ch246 p.1630).

question: A 20-yesr-old- man present with bilateral eye redness and watery discharge over the past few days. He denies trauma, vision problems, and fever. He says that the redness started in one eye then spread to the other. He also says he had a dry cough 1 week earlier that has resolved. Fluorescein examination of both eyes is negative for any uptake. What is the proper treatment?
options: Cool compress to the eyes and ocular decongestant
explanation: Allergic conjunctivitis: cool compresses and topical antihistamine/mast cell stabilizer (olopatadine) are first-line; topical antibiotics are for bacterial conjunctivitis; topical steroids have risks (Tintinalli 9th, Ch241 p.1583).

question: A 70-year-old woman complaining of headache on the right side of her head and visual loss in her right eye. She reported that she had intermittent headaches on both sides for several months, along with jaw pain while chewing food. On a physical exam, tenderness is noted in the right temporal region. Her visual acuity is only light perception in the right eye and 20/40 on the left eye. Other neurologic examinations and CT brain appear normal. What’s the best management for this patient?
explanation: 70yo woman with temporal headache, jaw claudication, ESR >50: giant cell arteritis — start high-dose corticosteroids IMMEDIATELY (before biopsy result) to prevent blindness; biopsy can be done within 2 weeks (Tintinalli 9th, Ch241 p.1591).

question: A 2-year-old boy presents with pain at left nasal pain. On presentation, he is alert and afebrile. Physical examination reveals a bead in the left nasal. What is the first proper management in this patient?
explanation: Nasal foreign body in toddler: 'parent's kiss' (parent blows into child's mouth while occluding unaffected nostril) is the least traumatic first attempt; Heimlich is for choking, not nasal FB (Tintinalli 9th, Ch242 p.1612).

question: A 60-year-old man presents with problems seeing out of his left eye over the past 2 hours. He denies trauma to the eye and pain. He states that he saw some floaters at first but now has some blind spots when he looks in certain directions. He denies any headaches. On examination, without dilation of the eye, his eye appears normal. Visual acuity is 20/20. What is proper management?
options: Advice it will be resolved in 24 hours and discharge
Urgent follow up ophthalmologist in 24 hours
explanation: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) presents with sudden painless monocular vision loss; emergency ophthalmology consultation is required within hours for possible intervention (Tintinalli 9th, Ch241 p.1594).

question: Fundoscopic examination as a picture. Which one is true about this patient?
options: Associated with hypercoagulable states, such as the use of oral contraceptives, hematologic disorders, factor V Leiden homozygous mutation.
An inflammatory condition affecting the small and medium-sized intracranial and extracranial vessels.
Most common in patients undergoing active treatment with immunosuppressing or chemotherapeutic agents.
CT scan done within 6 hours of onset can be normal.
An antihypertensive drug should be nitroglycerin.
explanation: Fundoscopic exam — central retinal vein occlusion (CRVO): associated with hypercoagulable states/hypertension; CT not needed within 6 hours for CRVO; not common in young patients (Tintinalli 9th, Ch241 p.1594).

question: A 40-year-old male with bipolar disorder presents with severe agitation, aggression, and auditory hallucinations. His wife states that sometimes he behaves dangerously towards her and others. His vital signs and general examination are within normal limit. What is the most appropriate initial pharmacological intervention?
explanation: 40yo male, bipolar disorder, severe agitation in ED: IM haloperidol — rapid effective antipsychotic IM for acute agitation; oral medications unreliable in agitated patient; IV diazepam as adjunct (Tintinalli 9th, Ch289 p.1870).

question: A 40-year-old male with acute psychosis exhibits a sudden onset of generalized rigidity, hyperthermia, sweating, tachycardia, and altered mental status. Which is the most appropriate treatment for this patient?
